L1.area: Smart pointer wrapper creation, conversion, and data projection mechanisms

L2.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in a Rust crate providing projected reference-counted smart pointers. Conversion functions that create projected pointers from standard library reference-counted types lack sufficient lifetime constraints on their generic type parameters. This allows projected pointers to outlive the data they reference. When the projected pointer is dropped, it can trigger the original reference-counted pointer's destructor at a point where the underlying data has already been freed, leading to a use-after-free.

L3.area: Non-thread-safe and thread-safe projected reference-counted pointer conversion and projection methods
L3.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists in a Rust crate providing projected reference-counted smart pointers. Functions that convert standard library reference-counted types into their projected equivalents, as well as methods that re-project existing projected pointers, lack sufficient lifetime constraints on their generic type parameters. This allows creating projected pointers that can outlive the original data. When such a projected pointer is dropped, it triggers the original destructor after the underlying data has been freed, resulting in a use-after-free. Both the single-threaded and thread-safe variants of the projected pointer type are affected.

ground_truth.reason: Conversion and projection functions on the single-threaded and thread-safe projected smart pointer types lacked 'static lifetime bounds on generic type parameters, allowing projected pointers to outlive the referenced data and causing use-after-free when the projected pointer was dropped.
